title: Controlled Vocabulary: Genre Depth Graph Edge Meanings
description: Canonical set of edge labels used across all 27 genre depth graphs. Every edge in every graph must use exactly one of these meanings. These represent refinement relationships — how moving deeper through genre levels narrows creative degrees of freedom and increases specificity.

edge_meanings.label: specifies constraint
edge_meanings.definition: The edge translates a broad expectation into a concrete, enforceable rule that the story must follow.
edge_meanings.typical_context: Level 1→2 transitions where a genre promise generates specific requirements

edge_meanings.label: narrows scope
edge_meanings.definition: The edge reduces the range of valid creative choices by focusing on a more specific pattern or requirement.
edge_meanings.typical_context: Any downward transition where options decrease and specificity increases

edge_meanings.label: branches into subtype
edge_meanings.definition: The edge splits into a recognized subgenre or variant that carries its own distinct constraint profile.
edge_meanings.typical_context: Level 2→3 transitions where core constraints generate distinct subgenre patterns

edge_meanings.label: mandates element
edge_meanings.definition: The edge requires a specific scene type, beat, or structural element to be present in the story.
edge_meanings.typical_context: Level 4→5 transitions where setting rules generate concrete scene obligations

edge_meanings.label: prohibits element
edge_meanings.definition: The edge forbids specific choices, tropes, or approaches that would violate genre expectations at this level.
edge_meanings.typical_context: Edges leading to anti-pattern nodes, or constraints that explicitly exclude certain techniques

edge_meanings.label: inherits constraint
edge_meanings.definition: The edge carries a parent-level constraint forward into a more specific context, applying it unchanged.
edge_meanings.typical_context: Level 2→3 or 3→4 transitions where a core rule persists across subgenres or settings

edge_meanings.label: sets tone
edge_meanings.definition: The edge establishes or adjusts the required emotional, atmospheric, or stylistic register.
edge_meanings.typical_context: Edges leading to tone marker nodes, or transitions that define mood requirements

edge_meanings.label: introduces setting rule
edge_meanings.definition: The edge adds a world-building or setting requirement specific to this genre context.
edge_meanings.typical_context: Level 3→4 transitions where subgenre patterns generate concrete setting requirements

edge_meanings.label: specializes threat
edge_meanings.definition: The edge makes the genre's source of tension, conflict, or danger more concrete and genre-specific.
edge_meanings.typical_context: Genres with strong threat models (Horror, Thriller, War) where threat type defines constraints

edge_meanings.label: restricts resolution
edge_meanings.definition: The edge constrains how the story can acceptably end, limiting valid outcomes for the genre.
edge_meanings.typical_context: Constraints that govern endings (Romance requires commitment proof; Tragedy requires downfall)

edge_meanings.label: differentiates from
edge_meanings.definition: The edge distinguishes this pattern from a related alternative, marking what makes this subtype unique.
edge_meanings.typical_context: Between Level 3 nodes where subgenres define themselves against sibling patterns

edge_meanings.label: requires payoff
edge_meanings.definition: The edge demands the story deliver a specific audience-facing reward that fulfills the genre contract.
edge_meanings.typical_context: Level 4→5 or 2→5 transitions where constraints generate testable delivery requirements
